extra.reasoning:
focal_weakness appears in only one rule: interaction_drop (priority 100, the highest) which sets acuity := self_care (the lowest level) when sweating AND rash AND focal_weakness. No rule ever raises acuity based on focal_weakness, so flipping it true can only activate this drop-to-self_care rule.

Counterexample, fixing sweating=true, rash=true, all others false:
- BEFORE (focal_weakness=false): matching rules are raise_sweating (p2 -> urgent) and raise_rash (p3 -> emergent). interaction_drop does not match because focal_weakness is false. Highest priority is raise_rash (p3) -> acuity = emergent.
- AFTER (focal_weakness=true, all else unchanged): matching rules are interaction_drop (p100 -> self_care), raise_sweating (p2 -> urgent), raise_rash (p3 -> emergent). Highest priority is interaction_drop (p100) -> acuity = self_care.

Setting focal_weakness true lowers acuity from emergent to self_care, the largest possible drop. This violates the monotonicity property ("setting focal_weakness true never lowers acuity"). The witness_assignment is the AFTER state (focal_weakness=true) with all other inputs held at the values used in the comparison.
